title: Mumps
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses

items.title: Impact of Vaccination on Mumps Incidence in the U.S.
items.content: Routine mumps vaccination significantly reduced incidence, but recent increases have been noted among vaccinated populations.
keyPoints: One-dose policy implemented in 1977 and two-dose policy in 1989 led to >99% reduction in cases by early 2000s.
Increase in cases since 2006, majority among fully vaccinated persons.
From January 2016 through June 2017, 150 outbreaks and 9200 outbreak-associated cases reported.
Since 2020, a large reduction in reported mumps cases likely due to COVID-19 prevention measures.

items.title: Genotypic Characteristics of Mumps Virus
items.content: Understanding the genotypes of mumps virus is crucial for molecular epidemiology and vaccine development.
keyPoints: Mumps virus is a paramyxovirus from the Rubulavirus genus, with one serotype.
SH protein exhibits hypervariability; SH gene nucleotide sequence (316 nucleotides) used for genotyping.
12 known genotypes designated by letters A to N (except E and M).
>98% of U.S. mumps virus specimens from 2015-2017 were genotype G.
Most vaccines globally use strains from genotype A, B, or N; U.S. vaccine uses genotype A strain.

items.title: Factors Contributing to Mumps Infection Among Vaccinated Individuals
items.content: Several factors contribute to mumps infection risk among vaccinated individuals, including immune response variability.
keyPoints: Failure to develop an immune response post-vaccination.
Development of a low-level immune response insufficient for protection.
Waning immunity over time after initial vaccine-induced response.
Lower levels of vaccine-induced antibodies to circulating wild-type strains than to the vaccine strain.
Lower frequency of subclinical immunologic boosting due to lack of exposure to wild-type virus.

items.title: Transmission and Infectious Period of Mumps
items.content: Mumps virus spreads through direct contact with respiratory droplets or saliva from an infected person. The infectious period extends from 2 days before to 5 days after the onset of parotitis, but viral shedding can occur as early as 7 days prior and up to 9 days post-onset.
keyPoints: Transmission via respiratory droplets or saliva
Infectious period: 2 days pre- to 5 days post-parotitis
Viral detection in saliva: 7 days pre- to 9 days post-onset

items.title: Pathogenesis and Dissemination of Mumps Virus
items.content: Mumps virus initially replicates in the nasal mucosa or upper respiratory tract, spreading to regional lymph nodes. Viremia facilitates dissemination to various organs including salivary glands (causing parotitis), testes (orchitis), CNS, pancreas, and others. The pathogenesis involves mononuclear-cell infiltration and tissue damage.
keyPoints: Primary replication in nasal mucosa or upper respiratory tract
Viremia duration: 3–5 days
Common dissemination sites: salivary glands, testes, CNS, pancreas, kidneys
Symptoms include parotitis and orchitis

items.title: Characteristics of Mumps Parotitis
items.content: Mumps parotitis typically presents with bilateral swelling, though unilateral involvement occurs in about one-third of cases. The condition is often preceded by a prodrome and lasts for approximately 5 days.
keyPoints: Parotitis is generally bilateral but can be unilateral in ~33% of cases.
Prodrome includes low-grade fever, malaise, myalgia, headache, anorexia.
Duration: typically 5 days (range, 3–7 days); resolves within 10 days.

items.title: Complications of Mumps in Postpubertal Males
items.content: Orchitis is the most common complication, occurring in up to 30% of unvaccinated and 6% of vaccinated postpubertal males. It can lead to testicular atrophy and potential subfertility.
keyPoints: Orchitis develops in ~30% of unvaccinated and 6% of vaccinated postpubertal males.
Occurs during the first week of parotitis, up to 6 weeks after.
Testicular atrophy occurs in ~30–50% of affected testicles.

items.title: Neurological Complications of Mumps
items.content: Mumps virus can cause CNS complications such as aseptic meningitis and encephalitis, with the latter having a mortality rate of ~1.5%. CNS involvement is more severe in adults.
keyPoints: Aseptic meningitis occurs in ≤1% of vaccinated and up to 10% of unvaccinated patients.
Encephalitis develops in ≤1% of patients, with a mortality rate of ~1.5%.
CNS complications more severe in adults than children.

items.title: Understanding Mumps Reinfection and Recurrence
items.content: Mumps reinfection, previously considered rare, may be more common than thought. It can occur after natural infection or as recurrent infection with swelling resolving and reappearing weeks to months later.
keyPoints: Reinfection and recurrence of mumps are possible.
Death due to mumps is exceedingly rare.

items.title: Differential Diagnosis for Parotitis Beyond Mumps
items.content: While mumps is the only cause of epidemic parotitis, other infectious and non-infectious causes must be considered. Laboratory testing can help differentiate between these causes.
keyPoints: Mumps is unique in causing epidemic parotitis.

Other causes include influenza A virus subtype H3N2, parainfluenza viruses, Epstein-Barr virus, herpesviruses, coxsackievirus, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, echovirus, lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus, and HIV.

Non-infectious causes include sarcoidosis, Sjögren's syndrome, Mikulicz's syndrome, Parinaud's oculoglandular syndrome, uremia, diabetes mellitus, laundry starch ingestion, malnutrition, cirrhosis, and certain drug treatments.
Unilateral parotitis may result from ductal obstruction, cysts, or tumors.

items.title: Why RT-PCR is Preferred for Mumps Diagnosis
items.content: RT-PCR is the preferred virologic method for confirming mumps due to its high sensitivity, specificity, and timeliness. It can detect mumps virus or viral RNA in various specimens including blood, saliva, urine, and CSF. Buccal swabs are optimal for detection.
keyPoints: RT-PCR is preferred over other virologic methods for its sensitivity, specificity, and timeliness.
Mumps virus can be detected in blood, saliva, urine, and CSF using RT-PCR.
Buccal swabs provide the best specimens for virus detection; parotid gland should be massaged before collection.
Maximal viral shedding occurs within 5 days after symptom onset.

items.title: Challenges in Serologic Diagnosis of Mumps in Vaccinated Patients
items.content: Serologic methods, such as detecting mumps-specific IgM antibodies or a fourfold rise in IgG, can aid in diagnosis. However, in vaccinated patients, the IgM response is often undetectable, transient, or delayed, making serology less reliable.
keyPoints: IgM detection is common within 5 days after onset and peaks around one week in unvaccinated individuals.
In vaccinated patients, failure to detect mumps IgM is very common due to an often undetectable, transient, or delayed response.
Collection of specimens >3 days after onset may improve IgM detection.
IgG testing is not recommended as elevated titers in vaccinated or previously infected individuals can prevent detecting a fourfold rise.

items.title: Optimal Vaccination Schedule for Mumps Prevention
items.content: The best prevention against mumps is vaccination, primarily with the MMR or MMRV vaccines. In the United States, children are recommended to receive their first dose of the MMR vaccine at 12–15 months and a second dose at 4–6 years. For adults at high risk, two doses are required, while one dose suffices for those not at high risk.
keyPoints: MMR vaccine is administered at 12-15 months (first dose) and 4-6 years (second dose).
Two MMR doses recommended for school-aged children and adults at high risk; one dose for preschool-aged children and low-risk adults.
A third MMR dose may be considered during outbreaks for those identified as at increased risk by public health authorities.

items.title: Effectiveness of MMR Vaccine Doses in Preventing Mumps
items.content: The effectiveness of the Jeryl Lynn strain-containing MMR vaccine is 72% after one dose and increases to 86% after two doses. A third dose during outbreaks provides an additional incremental effectiveness of 78%. The mumps component's effectiveness is lower compared to measles and rubella components.
keyPoints: One dose of Jeryl Lynn MMR vaccine has a 72% effectiveness (RR 0.24, 95% CI 0.08 to 0.76).
Two doses increase effectiveness to 86% (RR 0.12, 95% CI 0.04 to 0.35).
Third dose during outbreaks offers an incremental effectiveness of 78%.

items.title: Safety and Risks Associated with MMR Vaccines
items.content: The Jeryl Lynn and RIT 4385 mumps strain vaccines are safe, with no associated risk of aseptic meningitis. However, the MMRV vaccine has a twofold increased risk of febrile seizures in children aged 12–23 months compared to the MMR vaccine.
keyPoints: Jeryl Lynn and RIT 4385 vaccines have no evidence of aseptic meningitis risk.
MMRV vaccine poses a higher risk of febrile seizures (twofold increase) among children 12-23 months old after the first dose compared to MMR vaccine.

items.question: Which genotype of the mumps virus was most commonly found in the United States from 2015 through 2017?
items.explanation: Genotype G accounted for >98% of mumps virus specimens genotyped in the U.S. during this period.

items.question: What is the primary reason for using the SH gene nucleotide sequence to genotype the mumps virus?
options: It is resistant to inactivation by formalin and heat.
items.explanation: The SH protein shows hypervariability, making the SH gene sequence useful for genotyping.

items.question: What was the average annual number of mumps cases reported to WHO from 1999 to 2018?
items.explanation: On average, more than 500,000 mumps cases were reported annually during this period.

items.question: Which factor is NOT a reason for the risk of mumps infection among vaccinated persons?
options: Failure to develop an immune response.
Development of high-level immunity over time.
Waning immunity after initial vaccine-induced response.
Lower levels of antibodies to circulating wild-type virus strains.
items.explanation: The development of a high-level immunity is not listed as a risk factor; rather, low-level or waning immunity are concerns.

items.question: Where does primary replication of the mumps virus likely occur?
options: In the nasal mucosa or upper respiratory mucosal epithelium
items.explanation: Primary mumps virus replication likely occurs in the nasal mucosa or upper respiratory mucosal epithelium.

items.question: How is the mumps virus primarily transmitted?
options: Via airborne particles over long distances
Through direct contact with respiratory droplets or saliva of an infected person
items.explanation: Mumps virus is transmitted through direct contact with respiratory droplets or saliva of an infected person.

items.question: When does a person with mumps become most infectious?
options: From 5 days before until 2 days after onset of parotitis
From 7 days before until 9 days after onset of parotitis
From 2 days before until 5 days after onset of parotitis or other salivary gland swelling
items.explanation: A person is most infectious from 2 days before until 5 days after onset of parotitis or other salivary gland swelling.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms typically precedes parotitis in mumps infection?
options: High-grade fever, cough, and sore throat
Low-grade fever, malaise, myalgia, headache, and anorexia
Sudden onset of unilateral hearing loss
items.explanation: A prodrome of low-grade fever, malaise, myalgia, headache, and anorexia typically precedes parotitis in mumps infection.

items.question: What is the typical duration of parotitis in mumps?
items.explanation: Parotitis typically lasts for 5 days, with a range of 3 to 7 days.

items.question: Which complication is most common in postpubertal males with mumps?
items.explanation: Orchitis, or testicular inflammation, is the most common complication in postpubertal males with mumps.

items.question: Which of the following is a rare but severe CNS complication of mumps?
items.explanation: While encephalitis is a severe CNS complication, aseptic meningitis is rare and self-limited with no significant risk of death or long-term sequelae.

items.question: Which of the following viruses can cause aseptic meningitis that is clinically indistinguishable from mumps virus?
items.explanation: Enteroviruses can cause aseptic meningitis that is clinically indistinguishable from mumps.

items.question: What condition is the only known cause of epidemic parotitis?
items.explanation: Mumps is the only cause of epidemic parotitis.

items.question: Which condition can mimic mumps orchitis by producing a painful scrotal mass?
items.explanation: Testicular torsion can produce a painful scrotal mass resembling that seen in mumps orchitis.

items.question: Which virologic method is preferred for confirming mumps due to its sensitivity, specificity, and timeliness?
items.explanation: RT-PCR is preferred for confirming mumps because it offers high sensitivity, specificity, and timeliness.

items.question: What type of specimen provides the best results for virus detection in mumps?
items.explanation: Buccal swabs provide the best specimens for detecting the mumps virus.

items.question: When is maximal viral shedding likely to occur after symptom onset in a person with mumps?
items.explanation: Maximal viral shedding occurs within 5 days after the onset of symptoms.

items.question: Which strains are currently used in mumps vaccines in the United States?
items.explanation: The Jeryl Lynn and RIT 4385 strains are the only ones used in mumps vaccines in the United States.

items.question: What is the recommended age for administering the first dose of MMR vaccine to children?
items.explanation: The first MMR dose is recommended at 12–15 months of age.

items.question: What is the effectiveness of a single dose of Jeryl Lynn containing MMR vaccine in preventing mumps?
items.explanation: The effectiveness after one dose is 72%.

items.question: What additional risk is associated with the first dose of MMRV vaccine in children aged 12–23 months?
items.explanation: There is a twofold greater risk of febrile seizures with the first dose of MMRV vaccine.

items.statement: The mumps virus strain used in U.S. vaccines is genotype N.
items.explanation: The Jeryl Lynn strain used in U.S. vaccines is genotype A.

items.statement: Mumps was eliminated from the United States following the introduction of a two-dose vaccination policy.
items.explanation: Mumps is still endemic in the U.S. despite significant reductions due to vaccination.

items.statement: Mumps virus has been isolated from urine and seminal fluid up to 14 days after onset of parotitis.
items.explanation: The passage states that mumps virus has been isolated from urine and seminal fluid up to 14 days after onset of parotitis.

items.statement: Mumps encephalitis is primarily due to a direct cytotoxic effect caused by viral invasion of the CNS.
items.explanation: Mumps encephalitis appears to be more often a para- or postinfectious process rather than from direct cytotoxic effects.

items.statement: Mumps infection can be asymptomatic in ~20% of unvaccinated patients.
items.explanation: Approximately 20% of unvaccinated mumps cases are asymptomatic.

items.statement: Mastitis occurs in up to 30% of vaccinated postpubertal women with mumps.
items.explanation: The rate of mastitis among vaccinated postpubertal women is as low as ≤1%.

items.statement: Death due to mumps is common.
items.explanation: Death due to mumps is exceedingly rare.

items.statement: Mumps reinfection was historically considered common.
items.explanation: In the past, mumps reinfection was thought to be rare.

items.statement: A negative virologic or serologic result in a vaccinated patient with clinical signs of mumps rules out mumps infection.
items.explanation: A negative result does not rule out mumps, especially in vaccinated patients.

items.statement: IgG testing is recommended for diagnosing acute-phase mumps infection.
items.explanation: IgG testing is generally not recommended due to pre-existing elevated titers in vaccinated or previously infected individuals.

items.statement: A positive IgG titer guarantees immunity against mumps infection.
items.explanation: A positive IgG titer indicates exposure to the virus, not guaranteed immunity.

items.statement: MMR vaccine is recommended as postexposure prophylaxis for mumps.
items.explanation: MMR vaccine is not recommended as postexposure prophylaxis.

items.assertion: The majority of mumps outbreaks since the mid-2000s have occurred in populations with high two-dose MMR coverage.
items.reason: These outbreaks often occur in settings with intense or frequent close contact, such as universities and correctional facilities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why outbreaks have occurred despite high vaccination rates.

items.assertion: The mumps virus is inactivated by formalin and heat.
items.reason: This property allows for the safe production of vaccines using live attenuated viruses.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why these properties are beneficial for vaccine production.

items.assertion: Mumps-related hearing loss can be bilateral and permanent.
items.reason: Bilateral and permanent hearing loss are rare in mumps infections.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because mumps-related hearing loss is usually sudden, unilateral, and transient.

items.assertion: Orchitis in mumps can lead to permanent infertility.
items.reason: No studies have assessed the risk of permanent infertility in men with mumps orchitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason provided does not confirm or deny it; therefore, R is false.

items.assertion: Mumps is the only cause of epidemic parotitis.
items.reason: Influenza A virus subtype H3N2 can also cause an increase in parotitis cases.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why mumps is the only cause of epidemic parotitis.

items.assertion: A third dose of MMR vaccine is routinely recommended for all children.
items.reason: The duration of protection from a third dose may be short term (<1 year).
items.explanation: A third dose is not routinely recommended; it's considered during outbreaks.

items.assertion: Urabe and Leningrad-Zagreb mumps strain vaccines are associated with a higher risk of aseptic meningitis.
items.reason: There is no evidence of this risk for Jeryl Lynn and RIT 4385 mumps strain vaccines.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
